prompt:
Every service in our platform hand-rolls its CORS headers and no two agree — one echoes any Origin it sees, another sends allow-credentials with a literal *, a third forgets Vary and poisons the CDN cache. I want the decision logic in ONE pure module the gateway and the services can all call: cors.ts. No HTTP objects, no side effects — data in, decision out. TypeScript, node stdlib only.

Exports:
- compilePolicy(config): CorsPolicy — validates the config up front and throws TypeError on nonsense. config: { origins: string[], methods?: string[] (default GET/HEAD/POST), allowHeaders?: string[] | 'reflect' (default []), exposeHeaders?: string[], credentials?: boolean, maxAge?: number }.
- needsPreflight(req): boolean — req is { method, headers } where headers are the AUTHOR-set request headers.
- evaluate(policy, req): { type: 'passthrough' | 'preflight' | 'actual', allowed: boolean, headers: Record<string,string> } — headers use lowercase names, ready to merge into a response.

The rules I want pinned down, because these are exactly the things people get wrong:

Origins. Entries are exact origins ('https://app.example.com', 'http://localhost:5173', the literal string 'null'), the full wildcard '*', or ONE leading wildcard label like 'https://*.example.com'. '*' must be the only entry if present, and credentials:true with '*' is a config error — throw. Also throw on garbage patterns: no scheme, a * inside a label, 'https://*' with nothing after it, more than one *. Matching is case-insensitive over the whole origin string, but nothing smarter: an explicit port, a different scheme, or a trailing slash is a different origin. A wildcard entry matches subdomains at any depth, never the apex, and its dots are literal — 'https://*.intra.example' must not match 'https://box7.intraxexample'. The 'null' origin only ever matches a literal 'null' entry (or the full wildcard). Expose the check as policy.originAllowed(origin).

Preflight detection. needsPreflight is true when the method is anything but GET/HEAD/POST (any case), when any author header name falls outside the safelist accept / accept-language / content-language / content-type, or when content-type's media type (parameters stripped, case-insensitive) is not one of application/x-www-form-urlencoded, multipart/form-data, text/plain.

Classification in evaluate. No origin header → type 'passthrough', allowed, no CORS headers. OPTIONS with an access-control-request-method header → 'preflight'. Everything else, including OPTIONS WITHOUT that header, is 'actual'. Incoming header names must be treated case-insensitively.

Preflight evaluation. Origin must be allowed; the requested method is checked case-insensitively against the configured list; if the request carries access-control-request-headers, every name in it must be covered by allowHeaders (case-insensitive) — unless allowHeaders is 'reflect', which allows anything and echoes the requested list verbatim. On success: access-control-allow-origin echoes the origin EXACTLY as the client sent it (or a literal '*' for the pure-wildcard policy), access-control-allow-methods is the configured list uppercased and joined with ', ', access-control-allow-headers appears only when the request asked for headers (allowlist mode answers the CONFIGURED names in their configured casing), access-control-max-age only when configured, access-control-allow-credentials: 'true' only when credentials is on.

Actual evaluation. Allowed → allow-origin (echo or '*', same rule), allow-credentials when configured, and access-control-expose-headers joined from config when present. Method lists and max-age are preflight-only.

Refusals leak nothing: allowed:false and NO access-control-* headers at all.

Vary. Any policy that isn't the pure '*' policy varies by origin, so EVERY decision it produces — passthrough, refusal, success — carries vary: 'Origin'. The pure-wildcard policy never does.

test_cors.ts is the acceptance contract — node --test test_cors.ts green, don't touch the test file.
